item.title: Create ER Diagrams for PostgreSQL with a Free Design Tool
item.author: Dbschema Pro
item.content:encoded: Understanding a database starts with understanding its structure. For PostgreSQL users, one of the most effective ways to visualize and manage your schema is by using an Entity-Relationship Diagram (ERD). Whether you’re working with a large legacy database or starting something new, an ER diagram shows how your tables are connected and how your data is organized.In this article, I’m using DbSchema — a visual database design tool that’s free to use for creating diagrams.You can also generate HTML5 documentation (up to 12 tables in the free version) and explore features like Git integration with a 15-day trial of the PRO edition.We’ll cover two main workflows:Reverse engineer your existing PostgreSQL schema into an ER diagramDesign a new schema from scratchAn ER diagram (Entity-Relationship diagram) shows how tables (entities) in a database relate to each other through foreign keys (relationships). It typically includes:Primary keys and foreign keysRelationships between tablesOptional notes, indexes, and data typesUnderstand and explain the structure of a databaseIdentify missing relations or inconsistenciesOnboard new team members fasterCreate technical documentationKeep track of schema changes in large systemsYou can quickly understand how your tables are connected without digging through each one.A good ER diagram doesn’t just show tables, it shows structure, logic, and it makes your schema easier to explain to others, whether they’re developers, analysts, or non-technical stakeholders.What If You Already Have a Database?If you’re working with an existing PostgreSQL database (or another relational database), you don’t need to create the diagram manually.With this tool, you can your database and generate the ER diagram automatically. It reads your schema, identifies tables and foreign keys, and builds a visual layout you can explore and edit.Here’s an example of a PostgreSQL schema visualized as an ER diagram:Visual Schema Design (Without Writing SQL)Designing a database doesn’t have to start with CREATE TABLE.Using a visual tool helps you build clean, connected structures without syntax errors.Click to add tables and columnsUse forms to set types, keys, and constraintsDrag to create relationshipsSpot design issues before writing any SQLWhen you’re done, generate the SQL automatically.What Else Can You Do with Your ER Diagram?An ER diagram isn’t just something you generate once and forget. In DbSchema, it becomes a real workspace where you can organize, edit, track, and share your database structure.Use Multiple Diagrams to Stay OrganizedBig databases are hard to follow in a single layout. DbSchema lets you split your schema into multiple diagrams — for example, one for user data, another for inventory, and so on.This keeps things readable and makes it easier to focus on specific areas.Keep the Diagram in Sync with the DatabaseSchemas change — columns are added, constraints removed. The feature helps you spot the differences between your diagram and the live database.You can choose whether to update the diagram or the database, based on what’s changed.Version Your Diagram with GitYour diagram is saved in a project file you can commit to Git. That means you can track changes, collaborate with teammates, and roll back if needed, just like you do with code.Need to show the schema to someone who doesn’t know how to work with databases?It runs in any browser and doesn’t require installing anything. Also, you can save it as an SVG image, which is interactive and has a low size.Your ER diagram doesn’t have to be just a picture. In DbSchema, it becomes a tool you can maintain, track, and share throughout your entire project.Working with PostgreSQL’s JSONB columns visuallyPostgreSQL’s jsonb data type is a powerful way to store flexible, semi-structured data, especially when your records don't always follow a strict format. But querying jsonb fields can get complex fast, with operators like ->, ->>, and #>> cluttering your SQL.DbSchema simplifies this by allowing you to , using its built-in .View jsonb data in a table format, right from the data explorerBuild complex queries that extract keys, values, or nested structuresFilter and sort results without manually writing SQLUse expressions and conditions on jsonb fields just like normal columnsAdd Notes to Explain the ModelYou can add notes or comments directly to tables, columns, or relationships.This helps others understand your design choices, and the notes will also appear in the generated documentation.You can even use tags in your tables, which can be picked up later in automation scripts or deployment tools.Export for Reporting or DeploymentWhen it’s time to share your work or move to production, this tool gives you flexible export options: — Ideal for presentations, reports, or documentation — Generate CREATE TABLE, ALTER, and other scripts for deployment — Share a clickable version of your schema with teams or clientsThese options make it easy to document the schema in the right format for any audience, technical or not.Benefits of Using DbSchema for PostgreSQL ER DiagramsDesigning your PostgreSQL schema visually makes everything easier — from understanding complex relationships to avoiding structural mistakes early on.It’s also a huge help when working in teams or documenting your work for others.Whether you’re starting from scratch or improving an existing design, using diagrams and comments gives you a clearer picture of your database and how everything fits together.All of this in a free, visual, and easy-to-use environment.

item.title: 7 Frontend Design Patterns You Should Know (with Examples)
item.author: Batuhanmuzafferoglu
item.content:encoded:
When building frontend applications, especially with frameworks like React, Vue, or Angular, we often focus on features and visuals — but underneath it all, clean and scalable architecture matters just as much. That’s where come in.In this blog post, we’ll explore 7 essential frontend design patterns that can improve your codebase’s readability, reusability, and maintainability — with clear explanations and real-world examples.1️⃣ Container-Presenter PatternThis pattern separates logic and UI into two different components:: Handles data fetching, state, and side effects.: Focuses only on UI and rendering props.🧩 It helps keep UI components pure and testable, and promotes reusability.// UserContainer.jsximport UserCard from './UserCard';function UserContainer() { const [user, setUser] = useState(null); useEffect(() => { fetch('/api/user').then(res => res.json()).then(setUser); return user && ;}// UserCard.jsxfunction UserCard({ user }) {

{user.email}

);2️⃣ Higher-Order Components (HOC)An HOC is a function that takes a component and returns a new one with additional props or behavior.🧩 It helps abstract shared logic (auth, permissions, theming, etc.).function withAuth(Component) { return function ProtectedComponent(props) { const isLoggedIn = useAuth(); return isLoggedIn ? : ;}// Usageconst ProfileWithAuth = withAuth(Profile);3️⃣ Compound Components PatternCompound components are components designed to work together with shared internal state, usually via .🧩 It gives users a flexible API while hiding internal complexity. Content 1 Content 2Under the hood, Tabs.Tab and Tabs.Panel are wired via a shared context, so they know which tab is active.In this pattern, a component doesn’t render its own UI, but calls a function passed via a render prop to render something.🧩 It allows dynamic rendering and logic sharing between components.function DataFetcher({ url, render }) { const [data, setData] = useState(null); useEffect(() => { fetch(url).then(res => res.json()).then(setData);// Usage data ? : }⚠️ Note: In modern React, this pattern is often replaced by custom hooks.5️⃣ Controlled vs. Uncontrolled ComponentsThis pattern refers to and how their state is managed.React state is the source of truth.const [value, setValue] = useState(''); setValue(e.target.value)} />The DOM keeps the state, accessed via ref.const inputRef = useRef();🧩 Controlled components give better control for validation and behavior. Uncontrolled ones are simpler for basic use.6️⃣ State Reducer PatternThis pattern is useful for managing complex or customizable internal state — especially when building reusable UI components.🧩 It gives consumers of a component control over how internal state updates happen.function reducer(state, action) { switch (action.type) { case 'increment': return { count: state.count + 1 }; case 'decrement': return { count: state.count - 1 }; }const [state, dispatch] = useReducer(reducer, { count: 0 });7️⃣ Hooks Pattern (React)Custom hooks allow you to extract logic from components and reuse it across your app.🧩 To keep components lean and logic reusable.function useLocalStorage(key, initial) { const [value, setValue] = useState(() => { return JSON.parse(localStorage.getItem(key)) || initial; }); useEffect(() => { localStorage.setItem(key, JSON.stringify(value)); return [value, setValue];}// Usageconst [theme, setTheme] = useLocalStorage('theme', 'light');Frontend development is no longer just about HTML and CSS — it’s about building structured, reusable, and maintainable applications. These patterns offer proven solutions to common problems you’ll face as your app grows.Whether you’re a React developer or using another modern framework, mastering these patterns will help you write cleaner and more scalable frontend code.💡 Want a follow-up with advanced patterns like Facade, Atomic Design, or Event Delegation? Let me know!

item.title: Adding Secret Scanning and SAST to Your Projects in 15 Minutes
item.author: Vitor Hansen
item.content:encoded: Security should not be an afterthought in the development lifecycle. Whether you work solo or with a team, integrating security early helps prevent embarrassing and expensive vulnerabilities. In this article, you’ll learn how to add and Static Application Security Testing (SAST) to your repositories using , all in under 15 minutes.Every day, developers accidentally commit secrets (like API keys, credentials, tokens) and push vulnerable code. Attackers actively scan public repositories looking for these exposures. Worse: many teams delay security checks to the final stages of development. means integrating security earlier — ideally with every commit. — to automate our scans — for secret scanning — for SAST (detecting vulnerabilities in source code and dependencies) — for richer static analysis and code quality metrics1. Setup Secret Scanning with GitleaksAdd a file at .github/workflows/gitleaks.yml:name: Secret Scanning with Gitleaks push: pull_request: gitleaks: steps: uses: actions/checkout@v3 uses: gitleaks/gitleaks-action@v2 args: "--verbose"This scan will run on every push and PR to your main branch.Create a file: .github/workflows/trivy.ymlname: SAST with Trivy push: pull_request: trivy: steps: uses: actions/checkout@v3 run: | curl -sfL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aquasecurity/trivy/main/contrib/install.sh | sh -s -- -b /usr/local/bin run: trivy fs . --exit-code 1 --severity HIGH,CRITICALTrivy will analyze your codebase and fail the build if it detects high/critical vulnerabilities.3. (Optional) Add SonarCloud AnalysisIf you want deeper insights (like code smells, duplications, and maintainability), integrate SonarCloud.Create .github/workflows/sonar.ymlname: SonarCloud push: pull_request: types: [opened, synchronize, reopened] sonarcloud: runs-on: ubuntu-latest - uses: actions/checkout@v3 - uses: sonarsource/sonarcloud-github-action@master with: env: SONAR_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.SONAR_TOKEN }}⏱ Total Time Spent: ~15 minutesSonarCloud (optional): 8 minSecurity doesn’t have to slow you down. With just a few lines of YAML, you can:Prevent accidental secret leaksCatch vulnerabilities before they hit productionEnforce security on every pull requestThe earlier you catch issues, the cheaper they are to fix. So why not start now?

item.title: Deploy a full-stack web application on Kubernetes: A Full Course meal
item.author: Prabav Murali
item.content:encoded: Have you asked anyone in the tech community what Kubernetes is? Oh my bad! it is. If so, the answer would have been “a container orchestration tool” or “Ah! Its complicated to explain”. But, lets look at something beyond the definition. As a full course meal!P.S: Please have a custom web application ready with a frontend, backend with database operations before you start. It can be a very basic application.Before starting, let’s look at K8’s history…Kubernetes has its roots in Google’s internal , introduced between 2003 and 2004. Later, , Google released another project known as Omega, a flexible, scalable scheduler for large compute clusters. In that same year, McLuckie, Beda, and Burns set out to develop a “minimally viable orchestrator.” The desired set of essential features for the orchestrator included:: to deploy multiple instances of an applicationLoad balancing and service discovery: to route traffic to these replicated containersBasic health checking and repair: to ensure a self-healing system: to group many machines into a single pool and distribute work to themWhen it comes to deploying modern web applications, Kubernetes has a lot to offer ensuring scalability and reliability. Let’s go on to deploy the Full-stack application to Kubernetes using helm packaging tool. Helm is nothing but a packaging tool for Kubernetes in the form of charts which contains files that define the Kubernetes cluster resources (Basically making our life easier) :)Step 1: Containerize the React and Flask ApplicationsBoth the React frontend and Flask backend need to be containerized using Docker. Below are sample Dockerfiles for each component.Dockerfile for React frontend:In the below Dockerfile, I have chosen v20 from the LTS version (Stable) list. You can choose your desired node version by running ‘in shell/terminal. We do the to create a production ready application. (You can do a docker run after building the image)FROM node:20-alpine AS buildWORKDIR /appCOPY package.json package-lock.json ./RUN npm installRUN npm run buildCOPY ./build /usr/share/nginx/htmlCMD ["nginx", "-g", "daemon off;"]Dockerfile for Flask backend:So, I have used the slim image of python311 because thats the version in my local. But you are free to use the version you desire. Before you build the docker image, make sure you create the requirements.txt. The command for that is: ‘pip freeze > requirements.txt’. You can remove the unnecessary libraries from the requirements file (If you are not using a virtual environment)FROM python:3.11-slimWORKDIR /appRUN pip install -r requirements.txtCOPY . .CMD ["gunicorn", "-w", "4", "-b", "0.0.0.0:5000", "app:app"]Now, build and push the Docker images to your container registry (e.g., Docker Hub, AWS ECR). Here, I used Docker Hub.#frontenddocker build -t <reponame>/myfrontend:latest .docker push docker.io/<reponame>/myfrontenddocker build -t <reponame>/mybackend:latest .docker push docker.io/<reponame>/mybackendStep 2: Create a RDS MySQL database and access it with the Kubernetes ClusterAfter the database becomes publicly available (Check settings), Copy the RDS Endpoint and go to Security tab and click on the Security group which redirects to EC2 → Security Group and edit the . Add an entry to it to connect from MySQL workbench:Now, go to MySQL workbench and add a new connection. Paste the Endpoint address in Hostname, Username, Password and connect to the database. Now you can create tables according the web application requirements. We will do the same thing again after we create the Kubernetes Clusters.Step 3: Install Kubernetes, Helm and create templates for Kubernetes DeploymentIf you have Docker desktop, you can enable Kubernetes and wait for the engine to start running. Run to check if its installed. You can refer to the official Kubernetes documentation https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/tools/ for installation in other OS’es. Helm is an amazing tool! If you don’t have helm installed in your machine, you can refer to this URL: https://helm.sh/docs/intro/install/. If you are a Mac user, go to the terminal: ‘Now go to application directory and run ‘helm create <ChartName>’Create a directory for your Helm:This generates a basic Helm chart(s) structure, which actually halves the deployment work. We need to replace the default templates with customized Kubernetes manifests/values.Deployment for React frontend:templates/frontend-deployment.yamlapiVersion: apps/v1kind: Deployment name: {{ .Release.Name }}-frontend app: {{ .Release.Name }}-frontend {{- if not .Values.autoscaling.enabled }} replicas: {{ .Values.frontend.replicas }} {{- end }} matchLabels: app: {{ .Release.Name }}-frontend template: {{- with .Values.podAnnotations }} {{- toYaml . | nindent 8 }} labels: app: {{ .Release.Name }}-frontend #Pay attention to the matchlabel for later {{- with .Values.podLabels }} {{- toYaml . | nindent 8 }} {{- end }} {{- with .Values.imagePullSecrets }} {{- toYaml . | nindent 8 }} serviceAccountName: {{ include "myapp.serviceAccountName" . }} {{- toYaml .Values.podSecurityContext | nindent 8 }} - name: {{ .Chart.Name }} {{- toYaml .Values.securityContext | nindent 12 }} image: "{{ .Values.frontend.image.repository }}:{{ .Values.frontend.image.tag | default .Chart.AppVersion }}" imagePullPolicy: {{ .Values.frontend.image.pullPolicy }} - name: http containerPort: {{ .Values.service.port }} protocol: TCP {{- toYaml .Values.livenessProbe | nindent 12 }} {{- toYaml .Values.readinessProbe | nindent 12 }} {{- toYaml .Values.resources | nindent 12 }} {{- with .Values.volumeMounts }} volumeMounts: {{- toYaml . | nindent 12 }} {{- end }} {{- with .Values.volumes }} volumes: {{- toYaml . | nindent 8 }} {{- end }} {{- with .Values.nodeSelector }} nodeSelector: {{- toYaml . | nindent 8 }} {{- end }} {{- with .Values.affinity }} affinity: {{- toYaml . | nindent 8 }} {{- end }} {{- with .Values.tolerations }} tolerations: {{- toYaml . | nindent 8 }} {{- end }}templates/backend-deployment.yamlapiVersion: apps/v1kind: Deployment name: {{ .Release.Name }}-backend app: {{ .Release.Name }}-backend replicas: {{ .Values.backend.replicas }} matchLabels: app: {{ .Release.Name }}-backend template: labels: app: {{ .Release.Name }}-backend spec: - name: backend image: {{ .Values.backend.image.repository }}:{{ .Values.backend.image.tag }} env: value: {{ .Values.backend.dbHost }} value: {{ .Values.backend.rootUsername }} value: {{ .Values.backend.rootPassword }} - containerPort: 5000apiVersion: v1kind: Service name: {{ include "myapp.fullname" . }} {{- include "myapp.labels" . | nindent 4 }} type: {{ .Values.service.type }} - port: {{ (.Values.service).port | default 80 }} protocol: TCP selector: {{- include "myapp.selectorLabels" . | nindent 4 }} # <- Also look at the selector label here.apiVersion: v1kind: Secret name: {{ .Release.Name }}-mysql-secretdata: DB_HOST: {{ .Values.backend.dbHost | b64enc}} DB_USER: {{ .Values.backend.rootUsername | b64enc}} DB_PASSWORD: {{ .Values.backend.rootPassword | b64enc}}# Default values for myapp.# This is a YAML-formatted file.# Declare variables to be passed into your templates. # This will set the replicaset count. More information can be found here: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/controllers/replicaset/ # This sets the container image. More information can be found here: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/containers/images/ image: repository: docker.io/<reponame>/myfrontend # This sets the pull policy for images. # Overrides the image tag whose default is the chart appVersion. # This is for the secrets for pulling an image from a private repository. More information can be found here: https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/configure-pod-container/pull-image-private-registry/ imagePullSecrets: [] # This is to override the chart name. nameOverride: "" # This section builds out the service account. More information can be found here: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/service-accounts/serviceAccount: # Specifies whether a service account should be created create: true # Automatically mount a ServiceAccount's API credentials? automount: true # Annotations to add to the service account annotations: {} # The name of the service account to use. # If not set and create is true, a name is generated using the fullname template # This is for setting Kubernetes Annotations to a Pod. More information can be found here: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/annotations/ podAnnotations: {} # This is for setting Kubernetes Labels to a Pod. More information can be found here: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/labels/ podLabels: {} # Pod security context for the pod. podSecurityContext: {} # Security context for the container. securityContext: {} # drop: # readOnlyRootFilesystem: true # runAsUser: 1000# This is for setting up a service. More information can be found here: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/services-networking/service/service: # This sets the service type. More information can be found here: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/services-networking/service/#publishing-services-service-types type: ClusterIP # This sets the ports. More information can be found here: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/services-networking/service/#field-spec-ports port: 80# This block is for setting up the ingress. More information can be found here: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/services-networking/ingress/ingress: className: "" # kubernetes.io/ingress.class: nginx # kubernetes.io/tls-acme: "true" hosts: - host: chart-example.local paths: pathType: ImplementationSpecific # - secretName: chart-example-tls # - chart-example.local # We usually recommend not to specify default resources and to leave this as a conscious # choice for the user. This also increases chances charts run on environments with little # resources, such as Minikube. If you do want to specify resources, uncomment the following # lines, adjust them as necessary, and remove the curly braces after 'resources:'. requests: cpu: "250m" memory: "512Mi"# This is to set up the liveness and readiness probes. More information can be found here: https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/configure-pod-container/configure-liveness-readiness-startup-probes/readinessProbe: path: / initialDelaySeconds: 2 timeoutSeconds: 2 httpGet: port: 80# This section is for setting up autoscaling. More information can be found here: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/autoscaling/autoscaling: minReplicas: 1 targetCPUUtilizationPercentage: 80 # targetMemoryUtilizationPercentage: 80# Additional volumes on the output Deployment definition.volumes: []# secret:# secretName: mysecret# optional: false# Additional volumeMounts on the output Deployment definition.volumeMounts: []# mountPath: "/etc/foo"backend: image: repository: docker.io/<reponame>/mybackend tag: latest dbHost: 'Your RDS endpoint address' rootPassword: xxxxxxNote: It is unsafe/less secure to expose the password in the values file/ anywhere outside. Since, this work is for practice purposes, I went ahead with testing it directly. We can use AWS Secrets Manager or similar services to store the secrets and can be retrieved either in Dockerfile/Kubernetes Init containers. [HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]After everything is created, run the command:‘helm install <releasename> ./myapp — namespace <namespace>’.‘kubectl get pods -o wide’Now you will see something like ‘READY’:Now, let’s get the IP of the Kubernetes Cluster with the command: ‘kubectl get svc -o wide’. You will be able to find your IP of your release name. Copy that and go to security group (Please refer to Step 2) and add an entry to the ‘’. You can also choose the range of IPs you want to open to your database.After Ready status, you now need to port forward to test the application: Run the command:‘kubectl port-forward svc/<releasename> 8080:80’When you run this, you might get a timeout error which is intentional (Please scroll up and look at the podlabels in frontend-deployment template and selector label in service.yaml). But if you port-forward it to the pod directly, it will work. Why is that? Let’s see…‘kubectl port-forward <frontend-pod-name> 8080:80’Now let’s change the service.yaml to match the actual label of frontend-deployment.apiVersion: v1kind: Service name: {{ include "myapp.fullname" . }} {{- include "myapp.labels" . | nindent 4 }} type: {{ .Values.service.type }} - port: {{ (.Values.service).port | default 80 }} protocol: TCP selector: app: {{ .Release.Name }}-frontend # <- Changing it according to podLabel from frontend deploymentNow upgrade the helm with ‘helm upgrade <releasename> <chartname> — namespace <namespace>’ or ‘helm uninstall <releasename> — namespace <namespace>’ command and install it again. Then we run the ‘kubectl port-forward svc/myapp 8080:80’. It will workk!![] If you get something like ‘’, please check the cluster configuration/values/resource constraints or ‘’, then there might be an error with pulling the image from the repository. You can refer to: https://sysdig.com/blog/debug-kubernetes-crashloopbackoff/ for debugging tips.Now let’s introduce a Crashloopbackoff error to see how it looks.readinessProbe: httpGet: path: /notapath # <-Invalid path port: 80 periodSeconds: 10 livenessProbe: path: /notapath # <-Invalid pathRunning ‘ will give you the above result. Since, we changed path of readiness probe and liveness probe, the pod reached a crashloopbackoff state. The reason for that is the failure of health check causing repeated restarts. You can change it back and upgrade the chart again.That brings us to an end in the Kubernetes deployment where you have successfully deployed a React-Flask application. This is a dev practice session and to make it production-ready, we will be integrating Monitoring solutions, deploying to EKS etc. Stay tuned! Hey one more thing! Do stop and delete the RDS instance when you are done practicing to avoid lengthy credit card bills :D

item.title: Basic Docker Swarm Setup: Local Registry and High Availability (101 Guide for Beginners)
item.author: Worapon Asavanik
item.content:encoded: Learn how to set up a basic Docker Swarm cluster with 2 nodes (1 manager, 1 worker), configure a local Docker registry for sharing images, and deploy services with high availability (HA) and load balancing. By the end, you’ll have hands-on experience and the foundational knowledge needed to set up Docker Swarm confidently. This guide covers basic high availability at the application layer, using multiple service replicas across nodes. For full cluster-level high availability (including manager failover), you would need at least 3 manager nodes to maintain Raft quorum.This article covers:1. Overview: what is Docker Swarm?3. Building and tagging Docker images4. Setting up a local Docker registry and pushing images between nodes5. Initializing the Docker Swarm cluster and setting up replicas with load balancing6. Testing high availability at the application layer7. Summary of all commands1. Overview: what is Docker Swarm?Docker Swarm helps you group multiple Docker nodes to work together as a single cluster. With Swarm, you can easily deploy replicated services, balance traffic, and keep your applications running smoothly across nodes — all using Docker’s built-in tools.Swarm mode is an advanced feature for managing a cluster of . Use Swarm mode if you intend to use Swarm as a production runtime environment. If you’re not planning on deploying with Swarm, use Docker Compose instead. If you’re developing for a Kubernetes deployment, consider using the integrated Kubernetes feature in Docker Desktop.For more information on Swarm mode:dockerdocsA swarm consists of one or more nodes — physical or virtual machines running Docker Engine. handle cluster management, maintain cluster state, schedule services, and use Raft consensus for consistency. only run containers and do not participate in cluster decisions.For more information on Manager nodesand worker nodes:dockerdocs Two virtual or physical machines to act as Docker nodes (1 manager, 1 worker) for this guide, since . You can’t use only one machine if you want to test Docker Swarm with more than one node. If you need multi-node orchestration on a single machine, you might consider Kubernetes — I’ll cover that in a future guide, so stay tuned! Any containerized application (frontend, backend web server, or even databases — though for databases the approach differs, as this guide focuses on stateless applications) to test service deployment and load balancing in the Docker Swarm cluster.3. Building and tagging Docker imagesFirst, use PuTTY to connect into the host server that you want to make the first manager node.Note: If you cannot use PuTTY, follow these steps:sudo apt install openssh-serversudo systemctl status sshThen, go to your Dockerfile folder and run:For more information on how to create a Dockerfile,.sudo docker build –t <image_name> .# In my case, I ran:sudo docker build -t backend_test .The image is now being built.To make sure the image was installed successfully, run:You should see the image you just built4. Setting up a local Docker registry and pushing images between nodesFirst, I have two nodes: the and the .What is a Docker registry?A Docker registry is a service that stores Docker images. It lets you push (upload) and pull (download) images between machines. this is important because it allows all nodes (manager and workers) to share the same images without having to build them locally on each nodeNow, I suggest building a container that runs as a local registry to store images for sharing across your Docker Swarm nodes. This helps avoid complexity in the future.>>> Alternative (not recommended) approach: You could manually run sudo docker build -t <image_name> . on every host or node you want to include in the swarm. While this works, it doesn’t scale well in the long run like using a registry.Let’s run the local registry container:sudo docker run -d -p <your_host_port>:<your_container_port> --restart=always --name <your_container_name> <image_name>:<tag># In my case, I ransudo docker run -d -p 5000:5000 --restart=always --name registry registry:2Now our registry container is up.docker tag [local_image_name] [registry_host]:[registry_port]/[registry_image_name]:[tag]# In my case, I randocker tag backend_test 192.168.3.114:5000/backend_testExplanation of each part: → The name of the image that was built locally on your machine. → The IP address or hostname of the machine running the Docker registry. → The port number where the Docker registry is listening (usually mapped when you ran the registry container). → The name (repository name) you want the image to have inside the registry. → The image tag, like latest, v1, v2. If you omit this, Docker uses :latest by default.Push the image into the local registry:docker push 192.168.3.114:5000/backend_testYou might see an error like: “Get ‘https://192.168.3.114:5000/v2/”: http: server gave HTTP response to HTTPS client — this happens because the Docker daemon expects an HTTPS connection, but we’re currently using HTTP.Docker daemon configuration:Each node (manager and workers) must allow HTTP registries in daemon.json because Docker enforces security checks per node, not cluster-wide.We’ll begin by configuring the Docker daemon on the manager node (192.168.3.113).For Linux, the configuration file is usually located at:If the daemon.json file does not exist, just create onecd /etc/docker/sudo nano daemon.json# For example{ "insecure-registries": ["<your_host_ip>:<your_container_port>"]}{ "insecure-registries": ["192.168.3.114:5000"]}Then, restart the Docker service:sudo systemctl restart dockerNow, try to push the image into the local registry again.The result: we can push the image without the HTTPS error like before.You will see that the image has been successfully pushed into the local registry.At this point, the Docker daemon configuration on the manager node is complete. Let’s pull the image we pushed to the registry from the worker node (192.168.5.118). You can run a ping test to ensure the connection between 192.168.3.114 (manager) and 192.168.5.118 (worker) is working properly., let’s pull the image from the local registry:sudo docker pull 192.168.3.114:5000/backend_testYou will see an error message like in the following picture:Error response from daemon: Get "https://192.168.3.114:5000/v2/": http: server gave HTTP response to HTTPS clientdaemon.json file is usually located at: /etc/docker/daemon.jsonFollow the earlier steps and add the following line:{ "insecure-registries" : ["192.168.3.114:5000"]sudo systemctl restart dockerThen, try pulling the image from the registry again:sudo docker pull 192.168.3.114:5000/backend_testYou should now be able to pull the image from the local registry at 192.168.3.114 without the previous error.Now we’ve finished configuring daemon.json and we’re ready to initialize the cluster using Docker Swarm commands.5. Initializing the Docker Swarm clusterTo initialize Docker Swarm is pretty straightforward.First, pick the host you want to act as the and run:This command initializes Docker Swarm mode on the selected machine, making it the .The manager node will be responsible for managing the cluster, handling scheduling, maintaining the cluster state, and distributing tasks to worker nodes.At this point, we’re ready to move on because both the manager and worker nodes already have the same images.Next, copy the shown in the terminal, like:docker swarm join --token xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx 192.168.3.114:2377 Port 2377 is the default port used by the Docker Swarm manager for control plane communication, allowing worker nodes to join the cluster. You don’t need to configure it manually — it’s opened automatically when you run docker swarm init.Then, go to the worker node (in my case, 192.168.5.118) and execute that command in the terminal.# In my casedocker swarm join --token SWMTKN-1-4yjsq0tggpxlc5wceft9dac... 192.168.3.114:2377If everything works, you should see a response like:This node joined a swarm as a worker.Congrats! Your worker node has successfully joined the swarm.Now, switch back to the and check the cluster:You should see the worker node listed as part of the cluster.Finally, let’s check if any services are running:At this point, you probably won’t see any services yet — so let’s create one!To create a service (with 3 replicas), run:sudo docker service create \ --name <your_service_name> \ --publish published=<your_host_port>,target=<your_container_port> \ --replicas <number_of_replicas> \ <your_registry_ip>:<your_registry_port>/<your_image_name>:<tag><your_service_name> → The name of the service to create (e.g., backend_test)<your_host_port> → The port on the host (node) that external users will access (e.g., 8888)<your_container_port> → The port inside the container where the app is listening (e.g., 8888 or 7777)<number_of_replicas> → The number of container instances to run (e.g., 3)<your_registry_ip>:<your_registry_port> → The IP address and port of the Docker registry (e.g., 192.168.3.114:5000)<your_image_name>:<tag> → The image name and tag (e.g., backend_test:latest)sudo docker service create \ --name backend_test \ --publish published=8888,target=8888 \ --replicas 3 \ 192.168.3.114:5000/backend_test → The port on the host (node) that external clients connect to. → The port inside the container that the application is actually listening on.In Docker Swarm, the routing mesh automatically handles traffic distribution across all replicas of the service. This means you only need to send requests to the published port on any node, and Swarm will route the traffic to the correct container instance.You can optionally assign the service to a custom overlay network using the --network flag, like:--network my-overlay-networkIn this example, we didn’t specify a network, so Docker Swarm will use the default After the Docker Swarm service is created, let’s run:You should see three containers running, based on --replicas=3, distributed across the two nodes.Since Docker Swarm handles load balancing automatically, the manager node typically doesn’t take on many container workloads — its main role is to coordinate tasks. So next, let’s check the worker node.On the worker node (in my case, 192.168.5.118), you should see the other two containers running smoothly.You’ll also notice that all three containers are using the same port, thanks to Docker Swarm’s internal load balancing.At this point, you’ve successfully set up the Docker Swarm!5. Initializing the Docker Swarm cluster and setting up replicas with load balancingIn this section, we’re going to test if the requests are properly load-balanced across the replicas. On the other hand, we’ll also test high availability by forcefully stopping one of the containers and seeing if Docker Swarm automatically creates a new container to maintain the total of three replicas., I sent multiple HTTP requests using Postman, and as shown in the screenshot below, the requests were fairly distributed across all containers — confirming that the load balancing is working as expected., to test the high availability (HA) behavior, I went to the manager node (192.168.3.114) and ran sudo docker ps to see all three running containers. I then stopped one of them (with ID f65), leaving only two containers active. After that, I switched to the worker node (192.168.5.118) and ran sudo docker ps there. Docker Swarm’s HA mechanism automatically spun up a new container (with ID 949b2bc4bc42) to maintain the desired replica count of three.7. Summary of all commandssudo apt install openssh-serversudo systemctl status sshsudo docker build –t <image_name> .# In my case, I ran:sudo docker build -t backend_test .sudo docker imagessudo docker run -d -p <your_host_port>:<your_container_port> --restart=always --name <your_container_name> <image_name>:<tag># In my case, I ransudo docker run -d -p 5000:5000 --restart=always --name registry registry:2docker tag [local_image_name] [registry_host]:[registry_port]/[registry_image_name]:[tag]docker tag backend_test 192.168.3.114:5000/backend_testdocker push 192.168.3.114:5000/backend_testsudo nano daemon.jsonsudo systemctl restart dockersudo docker pull 192.168.3.114:5000/backend_testdocker swarm join --token xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx 192.168.3.114:2377sudo docker service lssudo docker service create \ --name <your_service_name> \ --publish published=<your_host_port>,target=<your_container_port> \ --replicas <number_of_replicas> \ <your_registry_ip>:<your_registry_port>/<your_image_name>:<tag>sudo docker service create \ --publish published=8888,target=8888 \ 192.168.3.114:5000/backend_testHave a nice day!Every clap means a lot and motivates me to keep sharing.Love you all, readers. Bye-bye! 💙

item.title: ChatLoopBackOff: Episode 63 (cloudevents)
item.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
item.content:encoded: Join us for Episode 63 on ChatLoopBackOff, where CNCF Ambassador Nitish Kumar dives into the world of CloudEvents for the very first time! In this interactive session, he will explore how CloudEvents is standardizing event data across services, enabling seamless interoperability in event-driven systems. Whether you're building serverless architectures, integrating across cloud platforms, or just curious about the future of event handling, this is your perfect intro. With live reactions, real-time learning, and practical experimentation, watch Nitish demystify the specification that’s powering modern cloud-native ecosystems—one event at a time.

item.title: Announcing the end of support for Node.js 18.x in AWS CDK
item.author: Charles Meruwoma
item.content:encoded: This change applies to all AWS CDK components that depend on Node.js, including the AWS CDK CLI, the Construct Library, and broader CDK ecosystem projects such as JSII, Projen, and CDK8s.Node.js 18.x reached its End of Life support on April 30, 2025, per the Node.js Release Schedule. This means the Node.js community no longer provides bug fixes or security updates for this version. By dropping support for end-of-life versions, we ensure that AWS CDK users benefit from the latest security patches, performance improvements, and modern Node.js capabilities. This approach aligns with AWS’s commitment to security best practices and our standard policy of supporting only actively maintained runtime versions.Starting December 1, 2025, AWS CDK will officially end support for Node.js 18.x. While your existing CDK deployments may continue to function, we will no longer address issues, provide bug fixes, or offer technical support for problems that occur specifically with Node.js 18.x. Any support cases or bug reports related to Node.js 18.x will require reproduction on a supported Node.js version (20.x or 22.x as of June 2025) before we can assist.Moving forward, projects that remain on Node.js 18.x will gradually lose access to new AWS CDK capabilities as we develop features using modern Node.js APIs that are not available in older versions. This creates a growing compatibility gap that will make it increasingly challenging to leverage CDK innovations and improvements. The security implications are equally concerning, as any vulnerabilities discovered in the unsupported Node.js 18.x runtime will not receive patches or workarounds from our development team, potentially exposing your infrastructure to known security risks.The challenges extend throughout the development lifecycle. Without regular compatibility testing against Node.js 18.x, we cannot ensure reliable CDK behavior, and you may encounter unexpected issues in production environments. When problems do arise, our support team will need to reproduce any reported issues on supported Node.js versions before providing assistance, which could delay resolution during critical incidents. Additionally, the broader CDK ecosystem, including third-party libraries and tools your projects depend on, will likely follow similar deprecation schedules, creating compounding compatibility challenges that become more difficult to resolve over time.We’re announcing this change in July 2025, to provide you with a five-month transition period before support officially ends on November 30th, 2025. During this transition window, our team will continue to address issues that arise with Node.js 18.x, giving you time to plan, test, and execute your upgrade strategy without immediate pressure. This period is designed to help you thoroughly validate your CDK projects against newer Node.js versions and ensure smooth deployments in your production environment.Beginning December 1st, 2025, AWS CDK will officially discontinue support for Node.js 18.x across all components and ecosystem projects. From this point forward, all bug fixes, security patches, and new feature development will target only supported Node.js versions, currently 20.x and 22.x as of June 2025. We strongly recommend using this transition period to migrate to Node.js 22.x, the current Active Long Term Support version, which will provide the longest runway for future compatibility as the Node.js release cycle continues.Version validation and update stepsBegin your migration by checking which Node.js version you’re currently running across all environments where you deploy CDK projects. Run `node -v` in your local development environment, CI/CD pipelines, and any automated deployment systems to get a complete picture of your current setup.Once you’ve identified all instances of Node.js 18.x, update your runtime to a supported version using either a version manager like nvm or by downloading the official installer from nodejs.org. We recommend upgrading directly to Node.js 22.x since it’s the current Active Long Term Support version and will provide the longest compatibility runway. After updating your runtime, thoroughly test your CDK projects in non-production environments to ensure your deployment scripts and third-party dependencies work correctly with the new version. Pay particular attention to any custom constructs or complex deployment workflows that may be sensitive to changes in Node.js versions.Finally, establish a process for staying current with future Node.js releases by bookmarking the AWS CDK Node.js Version Support Timeline, which provides up-to-date information on runtime compatibility and upcoming deprecations. This proactive approach will help you anticipate future changes and plan your upgrade strategies well in advance, avoiding the pressure of last-minute migrations when support windows close.This deprecation is part of our ongoing commitment to provide a secure, high-quality experience for AWS CDK users. By migrating to a Node.js Active Long Term Support (LTS) version, you will benefit from enhanced performance, ongoing security updates, and continued AWS CDK advancements. If you have any questions or concerns about this deprecation, please reach out and open an issue in our GitHub repo.

item.title: Kubeflow for enabling AI Powered Drug Discovery and development... Shrinidhi Venkataraman & Nithin R
item.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
item.content:encoded:
Don't miss out! Join us at our next Flagship Conference: KubeCon + CloudNativeCon India in Hyderabad (August 6-7), and KubeCon + CloudNativeCon North America in Atlanta (November 10-13). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io
Kubeflow for enabling AI Powered Drug Discovery and development in AstraZeneca - Shrinidhi Venkataraman & Nithin R, AstraZeneca
AstraZeneca’s robust AI platform, Azimuth—their first enterprise cloud-native machine learning platform—relies heavily on Kubeflow to power scalable and efficient AI workflows. In this session, we’ll explore how Kubeflow supports diverse AI use cases, with each project operating in its own dedicated namespace and persistent volumes ensuring durable data storage. We’ll cover how to enable cross-namespace volume access, build custom Kubeflow notebook images with VS Code and other editors, and use self-hosted GitHub runners to trigger pipelines. You’ll also see how integrations with tools like Grafana, ArgoCD, and Argo Workflows enhance the platform’s functionality. To maintain security and compliance, custom image governance is enforced through Kyverno policies. Finally, we’ll introduce the GreenOps framework—a set of practices focused on building sustainable AI solutions. Join us for an in-depth look at how Kubeflow powers enterprise-scale AI at AstraZeneca.
